FRACTIONAL CMO · WHAT TO EXPECT

First 90 days with a fractional CMO.

The single most-asked unspoken question from CEOs evaluating a fractional CMO: "What does this actually look like once they start?" Here's the honest week-by-week breakdown. The discovery work, the deliverables to expect, the leading indicators of success, and what to push on if it isn't working.

Days 1–14

W1–2Discovery

The first two weeks are about learning the business before changing anything. A fractional CMO who shows up with answers in week one is selling, not leading.

  • Stakeholder interviews: CEO, sales leader, customer success lead, finance lead, head of product, anyone who interacts with marketing inputs or outputs. 30-60 minutes each.
  • Data audit: CRM hygiene, attribution, GA, ad accounts, GSC, content library, marketing automation flows, vendor relationships. Note what's broken, what's missing, what's working.
  • Customer/prospect calls: 5-10 conversations with real customers and lost prospects. The single highest-signal activity of the first 30 days.
  • Competitive landscape: 6-12 direct + adjacent competitors. Positioning, content output, paid presence, hiring trends, recent fundraising.
  • What you should hear back from the CMO at end of week 2: Confirmed scope, list of known unknowns, draft current-state diagnosis.
Days 15–30

W3–4Current-state assessment + draft strategy

  • Written current-state assessment delivered. 5-15 pages. What's working, what's broken, what's missing, what the cost of inaction looks like. Honest, specific, dated.
  • Draft 6-month strategy. Top 3-5 priorities, sequenced. Where the budget goes, what gets built, what gets killed. The strategy should be opinionated — not a list of options for the CEO to choose from.
  • Team and contractor recommendations. Who to hire, who to keep, who to transition out (delivered privately to CEO, never publicly).
  • Quick-win identification: 2-3 things to start immediately that show momentum.
  • What you should walk away with at day 30: A written diagnosis and a draft plan you understand and agree with. If you don't have both, name it and address it immediately.

Day-30 honesty check: Can you articulate, in two sentences, what the fractional CMO believes is the single biggest constraint on your marketing function and what they're going to do about it? If not, the discovery wasn't deep enough.

Days 31–60

W5–8Strategy approval + first execution wave

  • Strategy finalized. CEO sign-off. Strategy documented in a living plan — not a one-time deck.
  • Team design. Org chart for the marketing function the strategy requires. Specific seats, levels, comp ranges. Hires identified.
  • KPI dashboard live. Weekly reporting against agreed metrics with a baseline. Leading + lagging indicators both.
  • Quick wins started. Typical first moves: AI lead-response agent live, content production loop standing up, one high-leverage paid channel test, broken attribution flows fixed.
  • Vendor evaluation. Audit existing agency/contractor relationships. Renew, restructure, or replace.
  • What you should see at day 60: Visible execution starting. Not just plans — actual deliverables, traffic shifts, lead-quality changes, team conversations.
Days 61–90

W9–13Working function

  • Marketing function visibly operating. Defined strategy, KPI dashboard reporting weekly, lead response under 60 seconds via AI agent, content cadence running, paid + organic channels with measurable lift starting.
  • Team in transition or in seat. New hires started, departing roles transitioned, agency relationships restructured.
  • First quarterly business review. CEO + (if applicable) board. Honest assessment of what's working, what's not, what's next.
  • Concrete plan for days 91-180. The 90-day plan transitions into a 6-month operating plan with sequenced priorities, dependencies, and accountability.
  • AI agent workflows producing measurable output. Lead response time data, content production cadence, reporting automation lifting team time back to strategy work.

Day-90 honesty check: Is the marketing function visibly different from day 1? If your answer is "we have a strategy doc and some hires but the operations haven't changed yet," push on it. Strategy without operating change at day 90 is a yellow flag.

If it isn't working

What to do at each checkpoint if you have concerns

Day 30 concerns

  • "I don't understand the diagnosis." The CMO should redo the assessment in plain language. If after a second pass you still don't understand, the fit is off.
  • "The strategy looks like generic best practice, not my business." Ask: "What specifically did you learn from our customer/prospect calls that drove this strategy?" Real answers will be specific.
  • "They haven't talked to enough of my team / customers." Push for the additional interviews now. A fractional CMO without ground-truth from your stakeholders is operating on assumptions.

Day 60 concerns

  • "Nothing is happening yet." Strategy without execution at day 60 is a flag. Quick wins should be in flight by now.
  • "The team isn't engaging." Often a process issue — the CMO needs more cadence, more visibility, more direct CEO endorsement of their authority.
  • "KPIs are flat." Normal at day 60. Leading indicators (lead response time, pipeline activity, content velocity) should show movement; lagging indicators (revenue) won't yet.

Day 90 concerns

  • "I still can't articulate what we're doing differently." If neither you nor the CMO can crisply state the change in your marketing operating model after 90 days, the engagement is in trouble.
  • "They keep blaming the team / the tools / the budget." Real fractional CMOs work the constraints they have. Blame externalization at day 90 is rarely cultural fit recovering.
  • "I'm spending a lot of my own time managing the engagement." The whole point is leverage. If you're spending more time on marketing oversight than before they arrived, the model isn't working.
What goes in the engagement contract

Define these milestones up front

  • Day-30 deliverable: Written current-state assessment + draft strategy.
  • Day-60 deliverable: Approved strategy, team design, KPI dashboard live, quick-wins in flight.
  • Day-90 deliverable: Working function with measurable leading-indicator lift; 6-month operating plan.
  • Cadence: Weekly CEO 1:1, monthly executive readout, quarterly business review.
  • Exit terms: 30-60 day notice either side; no penalty for cause; clear handoff expectations.
Want a walk-through of what Treetop's first 90 days actually looks like?
We'll show you a real engagement plan from a recent client (anonymized) and walk through the milestones.
